# Workflow-Aware Verifier Router Benchmark

**AI Provenance Notice:** This draft was generated by an AI system from automated research artifacts (run notes, decision JSON, benchmark outputs). The operator claims no personal authorship credit for the writing or results beyond releasing the artifact. Readers should treat this as an unreviewed AI-generated research artifact.

---

## Abstract

We investigate whether a verifier router that uses workflow metadata—workflow type, risk level, changed files, and acceptance tags—can select a smaller verifier set while preserving most of the defect-detection coverage of running every verifier. We implement a deterministic synthetic benchmark generating 420 workflow cases across seven workflow types with eight injected defect classes, and compare five routing strategies: run-all, workflow-aware, keyword-title, minimal-default, and random-budgeted. The workflow-aware router retained 97.4% of run-all defect recall (0.815 vs. 0.837) at 31.8% of run-all mean verifier cost (14.66 vs. 46.00 per case), with a false alarm rate of 2.3% compared to run-all's 13.6%. However, the workflow-aware router showed systematic miss clusters in integration-break, UI accessibility, dependency-risk, and configuration-break defect families. These results are limited to a synthetic, modeled environment and do not demonstrate production impact on real workflow traces.

## Introduction

Automated verification pipelines in software development workflows typically face a trade-off between coverage and cost. Running every available verifier on every change maximizes detection probability but incurs high cumulative cost. Selective routing—choosing which verifiers to invoke based on properties of the change—offers a potential efficiency gain, but only if the routing signal is sufficiently informative to avoid large coverage gaps.

A natural hypothesis is that structured workflow metadata (workflow type, risk level, changed files, acceptance tags) carries more routing signal than task-title keywords alone or a fixed default verifier set. This paper evaluates that hypothesis through a controlled synthetic benchmark.

The central research question is: **Can a workflow-metadata-driven verifier router achieve near-complete defect recall at substantially reduced verifier cost, compared to running all verifiers?**

We do not claim that synthetic results generalize to production. The benchmark models verifier detection probabilities rather than measuring them from real tools, and the workflow cases are generated rather than sampled from real repositories. Scientific closure for deployment requires replay against real workflow traces with actual verifier outcomes.

## Method

### Benchmark Design

We implemented a self-contained deterministic benchmark (`benchmark_router.py`) that generates synthetic workflow cases inspired by a software development workflow system (Enoch-like). Each case is characterized by:

- **Workflow type:** one of `code_change`, `ui_change`, `dependency_change`, `security_sensitive`, `data_pipeline`, `docs_research`, `config_only`
- **Changed files:** a list of file paths relevant to the workflow type
- **Risk level:** a categorical risk assessment
- **Acceptance tags:** metadata tags associated with the change

Each case has one or more **injected defect classes** drawn from: `unit_regression`, `type_error`, `visual_diff`, `security_issue`, `citation_gap`, `schema_error`, `repro_failure`, `config_break`, `integration_break`, `ui_a11y`, `dependency_risk`.

Verifier detection probabilities and per-verifier costs are encoded directly in the benchmark script. These are modeled values, not empirical measurements from real verifier tools.

### Routing Strategies

Five strategies were compared:

1. **run_all:** Executes every verifier on every case. Highest-cost baseline.
2. **workflow_aware:** Routes verifiers based on workflow type, risk level, changed files, and acceptance tags.
3. **keyword_title:** Routes verifiers based solely on task-title keywords.
4. **minimal_default:** Always runs lint and unit verifiers regardless of case properties.
5. **random_budgeted:** Selects 3 verifiers per case using deterministic random selection (seeded).

### Execution Protocol

A smoke test was run first (21 cases, seed 34), followed by the full benchmark (420 cases, seed 34). The full run was executed under `/usr/bin/time -v` to capture resource usage. Output artifacts were validated via JSON parsing and Python compilation checks.

### Metrics

- **Defect recall:** Fraction of injected defects detected by the selected verifier set.
- **Case detection rate:** Fraction of cases where at least one injected defect was detected.
- **False alarm rate:** Fraction of cases flagged as defective when no defect was injected.
- **Mean cost per case:** Sum of selected verifier costs, averaged across cases.

Bootstrap resampling was used to compute 95% confidence intervals for recall deltas and cost ratios between strategies.

### Environment

- Host: Linux gx10-efe8 6.17.0-1014-nvidia, aarch64
- Python: 3.12.3
- MemTotal: 127,535,908 kB; MemAvailable at start: 122,535,580 kB
- SwapTotal/SwapFree: 0 kB / 0 kB
- Full run elapsed: 17.50 seconds
- Max RSS: 20,544 kB
- Exit status: 0

## Results

### Overall Comparison

| Strategy | Defect Recall | Case Detection Rate | False Alarm Rate | Mean Cost/Case |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| run_all | 0.8367 | 0.9548 | 0.1364 | 46.00 |
| workflow_aware | 0.8152 | 0.9468 | 0.0227 | 14.66 |
| keyword_title | 0.6293 | 0.8777 | 0.0227 | 9.57 |
| minimal_default | 0.2483 | 0.5106 | 0.0682 | 5.00 |
| random_budgeted | 0.2222 | 0.4255 | 0.0455 | 12.68 |

The workflow-aware router achieved 97.4% of run-all defect recall (0.8152 / 0.8367) at 31.8% of run-all mean cost (14.66 / 46.00). Its false alarm rate (2.3%) was substantially lower than run-all (13.6%), though this comparison is confounded by the fact that run-all invokes more verifiers and thus has more opportunities for false alarms.

### Bootstrap Confidence Intervals

Workflow-aware vs. run-all:

- Recall delta mean: −0.0216; 95% CI: [−0.0310, −0.0125]
- Cost ratio mean: 0.3185; 95% CI: [0.3067, 0.3289]

Workflow-aware vs. keyword-title:

- Recall delta mean: +0.1854; 95% CI: [+0.1641, +0.2062]

Workflow-aware vs. minimal-default:

- Recall delta mean: +0.5673; 95% CI: [+0.5368, +0.5971]

The recall deficit of workflow-aware relative to run-all is statistically significant but small in magnitude. The recall advantage over keyword-title and minimal-default is both statistically and practically significant.

### Failure and Miss Analysis

The workflow-aware router exhibited systematic miss clusters in specific defect families:

| Workflow Type / Defect Class | Misses | Total Injected |
|---|---:|---:|
| code_change / integration_break | 26 | 30 |
| ui_change / ui_a11y | 19 | 35 |
| dependency_change / dependency_risk | 16 | 30 |
| config_only / config_break | 14 | 33 |
| security_sensitive / config_break | 11 | 34 |

These clusters indicate that the workflow-aware router's current routing logic does not adequately cover integration testing, UI accessibility, dependency risk analysis, and configuration validation. The integration_break cluster is particularly notable: 26 of 30 injected defects were missed, suggesting the router rarely selects integration-level verifiers for code_change workflows.

### Negative and Mixed Results

Several findings temper the positive interpretation:

1. **Workflow-aware does not match run-all recall.** The 2.2 percentage-point recall deficit (95% CI excludes zero) means some defects are systematically missed that run-all would catch.
2. **Cost reduction is not free.** The 68.2% cost savings come with a non-trivial coverage gap in specific defect families.
3. **Keyword-title is cheaper but much weaker.** At 9.57 mean cost per case, keyword-title routing saves more cost but at a recall of only 0.6293—a 25.0 percentage-point deficit versus workflow-aware.
4. **Random-budgeted performs poorly despite moderate cost.** At 12.68 mean cost per case, random selection achieves only 0.2222 recall, demonstrating that cost reduction alone is insufficient; routing intelligence matters.
5. **Run-all has the highest false alarm rate.** At 13.6%, run-all's broader verifier invocation increases false positives, which is a cost not captured in the verifier-cost metric alone.

## Limitations

1. **Synthetic benchmark.** All workflow cases are generated, not sampled from real development traces. The distribution of workflow types, defect classes, and their co-occurrence patterns may not reflect production reality.
2. **Modeled detection probabilities.** Verifier detection probabilities are encoded as constants in the benchmark script, not measured from real verifier tools. Actual verifier performance depends on code content, language, framework, and many other factors not modeled here.
3. **Modeled costs.** Verifier costs are abstract numerical values, not wall-clock times or resource consumption measured on real infrastructure.
4. **Single seed.** The full run uses seed 34. While the benchmark is deterministic and reproducible, different seeds may yield different case distributions. The bootstrap CIs partially address this but do not substitute for multi-seed evaluation.
5. **No real-world validation.** The results do not demonstrate that workflow-aware routing improves outcomes on actual software repositories or live development workflows.
6. **Defect injection model.** Injected defects are assigned to cases by the generator; the relationship between workflow metadata and defect likelihood is a modeling assumption.
7. **False alarm comparison is confounded.** Strategies that invoke more verifiers have more opportunities for false alarms; the false alarm rate metric does not normalize for verifier count.

## Reproducibility Checklist

- [x] Benchmark script available: `benchmark_router.py`
- [x] Deterministic execution with fixed seed (34)
- [x] Environment captured: OS, kernel, Python version, memory, swap
- [x] Resource usage measured: elapsed time (17.50s), max RSS (20,544 kB)
- [x] Smoke test executed before full run (21 cases)
- [x] Full run completed (420 cases)
- [x] Output validated: JSON parsing confirmed for both smoke and full summary files
- [x] Python compilation check passed for benchmark script
- [x] Per-strategy case-level results persisted as CSV
- [x] Summary statistics persisted as JSON and CSV
- [x] Execution logs preserved
- [x] Project decision and metrics recorded as structured JSON

## Conclusion

On a deterministic synthetic benchmark of 420 workflow cases, a workflow-aware verifier router retained 97.4% of run-all defect recall at 31.8% of run-all verifier cost. It substantially outperformed keyword-title routing (by 18.5 percentage points in recall) and minimal-default routing (by 56.7 percentage points), confirming that workflow metadata carries meaningful routing signal beyond what task-title keywords or static defaults provide.

However, the workflow-aware router exhibited systematic coverage gaps in integration-break, UI accessibility, dependency-risk, and configuration-break defect families. These gaps represent actionable design feedback: a production router should either include targeted verifiers for these families or implement escalation thresholds that trigger broader verification when workflow metadata indicates elevated risk in these areas.

The results are promising but not conclusive for production deployment. Verifier detection probabilities and costs are modeled rather than measured, and the workflow cases are synthetic. Scientific closure requires replaying the routing logic against real workflow traces with actual verifier outcomes, and measuring whether the cost-recall trade-off holds under real-world conditions.
